g.data-narration: Welcome to oceanic ecosystems - the largest and most diverse ecosystems on Earth, covering 71% of our planet's surface.
Oceans contain 97% of Earth's water, regulate climate, and support immense biodiversity from surface to deep sea.
g.text: • Contain 97% of all water on planet

g.data-narration: These key statistics highlight the critical role oceans play in maintaining Earth's ecological balance and supporting life.
Oceanic zones are classified by depth and light penetration - from the sunlit surface to the pitch-black trenches.
g.text: 🌊 Five Ocean Zones by Depth & Light

g.data-narration: The epipelagic zone is the sunlit surface where photosynthesis occurs, supporting most marine life and primary productivity.
The twilight and midnight zones have minimal to no sunlight, with organisms adapted to darkness using bioluminescence.
The abyssal and hadal zones represent Earth's most extreme environments with complete darkness and crushing pressure.
Marine food webs show energy flow from phytoplankton through multiple trophic levels to apex predators and decomposers.
g.text: Energy flows up • Nutrients cycle back

g.data-narration: Only about 10% of energy transfers between trophic levels, explaining why there are fewer organisms at higher levels.
Let's practice: if phytoplankton capture 10,000 kcal, calculate energy at each trophic level using 10% transfer.
g.text: Given: Phytoplankton capture 10,000 kcal | Transfer: 10% between levels

g.data-narration: Coral reefs are underwater cities supporting 25% of marine species while covering less than 1% of ocean floor.
g.text: 🪸 Coral Reef Ecosystems - "Rainforests of the Sea"

g.data-narration: Oceans regulate climate through thermohaline circulation, carbon sequestration, and absorbing excess heat from greenhouse gases.
Oceans face multiple threats: acidification, overfishing, plastic pollution, coral bleaching, and habitat destruction.
If the ocean absorbs 2.5 billion tons of CO2 annually, calculate the carbon stored using the conversion formula.
